content.text:
A **Diophantine equation** is an equation 
where we seek integer solutions. The simplest Diophantine equations are linear.

content.title: Linear Diophantine Equation
content.content:
A **linear Diophantine equation** in two variables is an equation of 
the form
\[
ax + by = c
\]
where $a$, $b$, $c$ are given integers and we seek integer solutions $x, y$.

content.text:
Not every linear Diophantine equation has a solution. For example, 
$2x + 4y = 5$ has no integer solutions because the left side is always 
even while the right side is odd.

content.title: Existence of Solutions
content.content:
The equation $ax + by = c$ has an integer solution if and only if 
$\gcd(a, b) \mid c$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $d = \gcd(a, b)$.

$(\Rightarrow)$ If $x_0, y_0$ is a solution, then $d \mid a$ and $d \mid b$ 
imply $d \mid (ax_0 + by_0) = c$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ By Bézout's identity, there exist integers $u, v$ with 
$au + bv = d$. Since $d \mid c$, we have $c = dk$ for some integer $k$. 
Then $x_0 = uk$ and $y_0 = vk$ satisfy $ax_0 + by_0 = auk + bvk = k(au + bv) 
= kd = c$.

content.text: Once we have one solution, we can find all solutions.

content.title: General Solution
content.content:
Suppose $(x_0, y_0)$ is a particular solution to $ax + by = c$, where 
$d = \gcd(a, b)$. Then all integer solutions are given by
\[
x = x_0 + \frac{b}{d}t, \qquad y = y_0 - \frac{a}{d}t
\]
where $t$ ranges over all integers.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $(x_1, y_1)$ be any solution. Then $ax_0 + by_0 = c = ax_1 + by_1$, so
\[
a(x_1 - x_0) = b(y_0 - y_1).
\]
Dividing by $d$: $(a/d)(x_1 - x_0) = (b/d)(y_0 - y_1)$.

Since $\gcd(a/d, b/d) = 1$, we have $(b/d) \mid (x_1 - x_0)$. Write 
$x_1 - x_0 = (b/d)t$ for some integer $t$. Then 
$y_0 - y_1 = (a/d)t$, giving the stated formulas.

Conversely, any $(x, y)$ of the stated form satisfies $ax + by = c$.

content.title: Solving a Linear Diophantine Equation
content.solution:
First, $\gcd(15, 6) = 3$, and $3 \mid 33$, so solutions exist.

Dividing by 3: $5x + 2y = 11$.

By inspection or the extended Euclidean algorithm, one solution is 
$x_0 = 1$, $y_0 = 3$ (check: $5(1) + 2(3) = 11$).

The general solution is:
\[
x = 1 + 2t, \qquad y = 3 - 5t
\]
for any integer $t$.

content.text: Often we want only positive solutions.

content.title: Positive Solutions
content.problem: Find all positive integer solutions to $5x + 7y = 100$.
content.solution:
Since $\gcd(5, 7) = 1 \mid 100$, solutions exist. One particular solution 
is $x_0 = 20$, $y_0 = 0$ (not positive). Another is $x_0 = 6$, $y_0 = 10$ 
(check: $5(6) + 7(10) = 100$).

General solution: $x = 6 + 7t$, $y = 10 - 5t$.

For positive solutions: $x > 0 \Rightarrow t > -6/7$, so $t \geq 0$. 
And $y > 0 \Rightarrow t < 2$, so $t \leq 1$.

Thus $t \in \{0, 1\}$, giving solutions $(6, 10)$ and $(13, 5)$.
